coredata.dc:description: Osteosarcoma as an aggressive malignant neoplasm is commonly treated by surgical resection, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy, but surgery induces bone defects and chemo/radiotherapy causes severe systemic toxicity. We have designed a novel low-toxic bifunctional 0D/2D heterojunction (HJ) that can be used to generate effective photothermal effects in the second NIR window (NIR-II, 1000–1350 nm) for simultaneous osteosarcoma treatment and bone regeneration. To construct this 0D/2D nanostructure, biocompatible, positively charged NIR-II-responsive carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by an ultrafast microwave-assisted hydrothermal method, followed by electrostatically driven assembly on both sides of negatively charged WS2 nanosheets. We found that the HJs exhibited enhanced NIR-II absorption and photothermal conversion efficiency. Due to the deep-tissue penetration ability of 1064 nm laser, complete osteosarcoma ablation was realized at a low power density even when being covered by a 10-mm-thick additional tissue. Furthermore, osteogenic differentiation was remarkably enhanced by mild photothermal effects of CD/WS2 HJs, which significantly up-regulated bone-related gene expressions, particularly, heat shock protein expression. Designed CD/WS2 HJs can be used in osteosarcoma treatment and bone-tissue engineering.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM, HRTEM and AFM images of NIR-II responsive CDs (a-c), WS2 nanosheets (d-f), and CD/WS2 HJs (g-i).

(a, b) XRD patterns of WS2 nanosheets and CD/WS2 HJs. (c, d) Survey XPS spectra of WS2 nanosheets and CD/WS2 HJs. (e-i) High-resolution W 4f spectrum, high-resolution S 2p spectrum, high-resolution C 1 s spectrum, high-resolution N 1 s spectrum, and high-resolution O 1 s spectrum of CD/WS2 HJs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs and CD/WS2 HJs. (b) UV–vis-NIR absorption spectra of CDs, WS2, and CD/WS2 HJs. (c) Schematic Illustration of the energy position and the deep defect state formation under NIR laser irradiation. (d) Temperature elevation of CDs, WS2, and CD/WS2 HJs at the same concentrations (600 μg mL−1) as a function of time under 1064 nm laser irradiation (0.6 W cm−2). (e) Temperature elevation of pure water and CD/WS2 HJs with varied concentrations as a function of irradiation time under 1064 nm laser irradiation (0.6 W cm−2). (f) Temperature elevation of CD/WS2 HJs under 1064 nm laser irradiation at varied power densities as a function of time. (g) Temperature variation of CD/WS2 HJs over several on/off cycles of 1064 nm laser irradiation. (h) The photothermal effect of CDs, WS2, and CD/WS2 HJs under 1064 nm laser irradiation (0.6 W cm−2). (i) Plot of the cooling time versus the negative natural logarithm of the temperature driving force obtained from the cooling period. (j) Infrared thermographic images of centrifuge tubes filled with CDs, WS2, and CD/WS2 HJs at the same concentrations (600 μg mL−1) were measured at 0–5 min with an IR camera. (k) Schematic illustration of deep-tissue photothermal study. (l) Evaluation of the tissue-thickness-dependent photothermal effect of the CD/WS2 HJs after 808 or 1064 nm laser passed through chicken breast tissue of various thicknesses (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 mm).

(a) CT images of CD/WS2 HJ solutions with different concentrations (0–10 mg mL−1). (b) HU values of CD/WS2 HJs as a function of its concentrations. (c) CT images of mice before and 24 h after i.v. injection with CD/WS2 HJs (1.0 mg mL−1, 200 μL).

(a) Tumor growth curves of mice after receiving different treatments via intravenous injection. (b) Schematic illustration of deep tissue PTT. A piece of 10 mm chicken breast tissue was placed on top of the tumor during laser irradiation. (c) Tumor growth curves of mice after receiving different deep tissue PTT treatments via intravenous injection. (d) Pictures of tumors obtained from mice on day 18 after receiving different treatments. (e) Representative photographs of mice bearing MG-63 tumors on 18 days after different treatments. The tumor was marked with red circles. (f) Monitoring curves of the body weight of mice over the whole treatment period. (g) Maximum temperature within the tumor region recorded by an IR camera after intravenous injection of saline or CD/WS2 HJs upon 1064 nm laser irradiation (0.6 W cm−2).

(a) Effects of different samples on the ALP activity of osteoblasts. Results are mean ± SD of the triplicate experiments. (b) Quantification of the stained mineral deposits upon their extraction. (c) Digital images of alizarin red S stained calcium-rich deposits. (d) Images of alizarin red stained calcium nodules from hMSCs cultured with WS2, CD/WS2, WS2 + laser, and CD/WS2 + laser, respectively. (e) Western blotting analysis of HSP-70, OCN, RUNX2, COL-1, and β-catenin. GAPDH was used as the loading control.

Preparation procedure of CD/WS2 HJs including three steps. Step 1: the microwave-assisted synthesis of NIR-II responsive CDs. Step 2: WS2 nanosheets were synthesized via a liquid exfoliation method. Step 3: CD/WS2 HJs were prepared through a simple electrostatic interaction.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot/WS2 heterojunctions for NIR-II enhanced photothermal therapy of osteosarcoma and bone regeneration

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Osteosarcoma as an aggressive malignant neoplasm is commonly treated by surgical resection, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy, but surgery induces bone defects and chemo/radiotherapy causes severe systemic toxicity. We have designed a novel low-toxic bifunctional 0D/2D heterojunction (HJ) that can be used to generate effective photothermal effects in the second NIR window (NIR-II, 1000–1350 nm) for simultaneous osteosarcoma treatment and bone regeneration. To construct this 0D/2D nanostructure, biocompatible, positively charged NIR-II-responsive carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by an ultrafast microwave-assisted hydrothermal method, followed by electrostatically driven assembly on both sides of negatively charged WS2 nanosheets. We found that the HJs exhibited enhanced NIR-II absorption and photothermal conversion efficiency. Due to the deep-tissue penetration ability of 1064 nm laser, complete osteosarcoma ablation was realized at a low power density even when being covered by a 10-mm-thick additional tissue. Furthermore, osteogenic differentiation was remarkably enhanced by mild photothermal effects of CD/WS2 HJs, which significantly up-regulated bone-related gene expressions, particularly, heat shock protein expression. Designed CD/WS2 HJs can be used in osteosarcoma treatment and bone-tissue engineering.

ce:section.ce:para: Osteosarcoma is an aggressive malignant neoplasm that is most prevalent in teenagers and young adults [1,2]. In the clinic, surgical resection is commonly used to treat osteosarcoma [3–5]. However, incomplete bone-tumor cell elimination and the formation of bone defects seriously hinder the clinic application of surgical intervention [6,7]. Adjuvant chemotherapy and radiotherapy can kill residual tumor cells after surgical resection, but drug resistance and considerable systemic side effects may result in tumor recurrence and endless suffering to patients [8–11]. Therefore, it remains a significant challenge to develop novel effective combined therapeutic strategies for killing osteosarcoma cells and repairing bone defects arising from surgical resection.

Compared with chemotherapy and radiotherapy, photothermal cancer therapy (PTT) has been extensively considered as a minimally invasive therapeutic strategy to achieve the desired level of efficacy and avoid damage to normal tissues [12–17]. Moreover, photothermal effects can also effectively controlled for bone regeneration, because mild photothermal stimulation (1.5–3.0 °C above normal) not only greatly enhances proliferation of human mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs) but also promotes osteogenesis [18,19]. To maximize the photothermal effects for effective osteosarcoma treatment and bone regeneration in deep tissues, it is more desirable to develop PTT agents that can be effectively performed in the second NIR window (NIR-II, 1000–1350 nm) rather than the first NIR window (NIR-I, 700–950 nm) [20-25].

Currently, NIR-I responsive bifunctional biomaterials have been extensively developed as PTT agents for osteosarcoma treatment or/and as bone regenerating agents for bone repair, such as black phosphorus nanosheets [26,27], graphene oxide [28,29], copper-based chalcogenides [30], and organic nanosystems [31,32]. However, their clinic applications to osteosarcoma treatment and bone regeneration are limited, because osteosarcoma is buried in a deep tissue. No design has been made to utilize NIR-II responsive bifunctional agents for generating effective photothermal effects in the NIR-II window for osteosarcoma treatment and bone regeneration.

In this work, we designed hybrid 0D/2D heterojunctions (HJs) for PTT of osteosarcoma and photothermally enhanced bone regeneration using biocompatible carbon dots (CDs) as a main NIR-II agent and WS2 nanosheets as a supplementary NIR-II agent, a CD carrier, and a high CT contrast agent. Although both CDs and WS2 nanosheets have photothermal properties that can be extended to the NIR-II window for PTT, each has intrinsic disadvantages that could impede their direct biomedicine applications. For WS2 nanosheets only, they showed obvious agglomeration in physiological solutions, such as PBS, DMEM, and blood. Also, their rather low photothermal conversion efficiency in the NIR-II window severely hindered their potential applications in deep-tissue PTT. For CDs only, albeit their high stability in physiological solutions and excellent photothermal properties in the NIR-II window, the tiny nanoparticles with diameters as small as ~10 nm showed nonspecificity to the tumor tissue due to rapid excretion via hepatobiliary and kidney systems. The constructed CD/WS2 heterojunctions can not only utilize the advantages of the two components but also overcome their disadvantages for osteosarcoma treatment and photothermally enhanced bone regeneration based on the following new effects:

Firstly, compared with CDs or WS2 only, the CD/WS2 heterojunctions exhibited synergistically enhanced optical absorption and a high total photothermal conversion efficiency in the NIR-II window. Secondly, by virtue of the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect of the WS2 nanosheet component, the heterojunctions with markedly increased size greatly improved the targeting ability of the NIR-II-responsive CD component in the tumor tissues through intravenous injection, enabling realization of complete tumor recession at rather low laser exposure (0.6 W cm−2 for 1064 nm). Thirdly, the CD/WS2 heterojunctions exhibited significantly enhanced physiological stability, without showing any agglomeration in PBS, DMEM culture medium, and blood. Fourthly, the CT imaging-guided PTT was achieved by taking advantage of the strong X-ray attenuation capacity of WS2 nanosheets. Finally, osteogenic differentiation was remarkably enhanced by mild photothermal effects of CD/WS2 HJs, which significantly up-regulated bone-related gene expressions, particularly, heat shock protein expression. Because of the enhanced photothermal effects, improved tumor targeting ability and increased physiological stability, designed CD/WS2 HJs can be thus used in osteosarcoma treatment and bone-tissue engineering.

1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) was first prepared through the nitration reaction of pyrene (TCI, Japan, purity >98%) following our previous report [33]. NIR-II-responsive CDs were synthesized in a microwave instrument (Anton Paar Monowave 400) using TNP and BPEI (Aladdin, China, Mn ~ 10000) as the precursors. Typically, TNP and BPEI (BPEI:TNP = 1:16) were dispersed in 10 mL DI water by sonication to form a dispersion. The dispersions were then transferred into a 10 mL microwave-dedicated reactor glass tube and heated at 230 °C for 5 min. After a rapid microwave reaction, the formed CD solutions were filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membranes to remove insoluble carbon and dialyzed in a 3500 Da dialysis bag for two days to remove soluble small molecules.

Commercial WS2 powder (Aladdin, China) was first ground for 3 h in the ball grinder (MITR-YXQM-2L). Then, 50 mg of WS2 was dispersed in H2SO4 (50 mL) and heated at 90 °C for 24 h. The residual H2SO4 was removed through centrifugation (6000 rpm, 10 min). Finally, the products were dispersed in 40 mL of DI water by sonication for 2 h to obtain the single-layer WS2 nanosheets.

The CD/WS2 HJs were synthesized by assembling the CDs (0.4 mg mL−1) and WS2 nanosheets (0.2 mg mL−1) under vigorous stirring at room temperature in PBS (20 mM) for 24 h. Excess unbounded CDs were washed away by centrifugation (9000 rpm, 15 min) and repeatedly for several times. The obtained CD/WS2 HJs were dispersed in water for future use. The loading ratio of CDs were determined by the changed mass of WS2 nanosheets before and after CD loading. Herein, 0.4 mg CDs can be almost completely loaded on the WS2 nanosheets (0.2 mg).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the sample were recorded on a JEM-2100F microscope operated at 200 kV acceleration voltage using super thin carbon films. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained with a Rigaku 18 KW D/max-2550 using Cu Kα radiation. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of dried samples were collected using a Nicolet AVATAR 370 FT-IR. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The zeta potential and size of the sample were recorded by a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 Zeta potential analyzer. UV–vis-NIR absorption spectra were obtained by Agilent Cary 5000 spectrophotometers and fluorescence spectra were carried out using a Hitachi 7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

Photothermal properties of CD/WS2 HJs were investigated by irradiating a centrifuge tube containing a dispersion of CD/WS2 HJs at varied concentrations (0, 300, 400, 500, and 600 μg mL−1). CD/WS2 HJ aqueous solutions were irradiated with the 1064 nm laser for 5 min. We used the Fluke infrared thermal imaging camera to measure the temperature of CD/WS2 HJ solution every 30 s. The photothermal performance of pristine WS2 and CDs were also investigated under the same irradiation condition. Photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of CD/WS2 HJs was determined according to the following Eq. (1):

Therefore, the time constant (τs ) was determined to be 383.8 and 160.9 s for 808 and 1064 nm, respectively. Then, the η at 808 and 1064 nm of CD/WS2 can be calculated to be 66.6 and 56.3%, respectively.

The MG-63 osteosarcoma cells were obtained from the Chinese Academy of Sciences. MTT assays were performed to evaluate the cytotoxicity of CDs, WS2, and CD/WS2 against MG-63 cells. First, MG-63 cells were cultured in the 96-well plates. After incubated at 37 °C overnight, CD/WS2 HJs with varied concentrations (37.5, 75, 150, 300 and 600 μg mL−1) were added. Then, MTT (20 μL) was added into the wells. Finally, the medium was removed after another 4 h incubation and DMSO (150 μL) was added into the cells. The cell viabilities were determined by measuring the 490 nm absorbance.

The cellular uptake of CD/WS2 HJs was investigated using quantitative flow cytometry. Briefly, MG-63 cells were seeded on 24-well plates and incubated for 24 h. Following treatment with CD/WS2 for 2 h at 37 °C, cells were washed with PBS and the fluorescence intensity of CD/WS2 was measured on a flow cytometer (BD FACS Calibur, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro PTT in the NIR-II window
ce:section.ce:para: The PTT efficiency were evaluated by measuring the cell viabilities of MG-63 cells in the presence of CD/WS2 HJs upon laser irradiation (1064 nm, 0.6 W cm−2) using MTT assays. First, MG-63 cells were cultured in the 96-well plates. After incubated at 37 °C overnight, CD/WS2 HJs with varied concentrations (37.5, 75, 150, 300 and 600 μg mL−1) were added. Then, we used the 1064 nm laser to irradiate the MG-63 cells for 5 min. Finally, MTT assay was performed after incubation for an additional 20 h to evaluate the PTT efficiency of CD/WS2 HJs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo CT imaging
ce:section.ce:para: All animal experiments were conducted in compliance with “The National Regulation of China for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals” and approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Shanghai University. 100 μL of MG-63 cell suspension (5 × 106 cells) were subcutaneously injected into the axillary of Balb/c nude mice (SLAC Laboratory Animal Co. Ltd., China). The CT imaging was conducted on a SkyScan 1176 high resolution in-vivo X-ray microtomograph (Bruker, Germany). For in vitro CT imaging, CD/WS2 HJs were dispersed in DI water and then managed in a line for CT imaging measurements. For in vivo CT imaging, CD/WS2 suspensions (5.0 mg kg−1) was intravenously injected into MG-63 tumor-bearing mice, and the whole mice was imaged at 0 and 24 h post-injection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biodistribution of CD/WS2 HJs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the biodistribution of CD/WS2 HJs, mice bearing MG-63 tumors were intravenously injected with CD/WS2. At 0, 6, 12, 24, and 48 h post-injection, the major organs and tumors were harvested and imaged using IVIS Lumina III in vivo Imaging System (PerkinElmer, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo PTT in the NIR-I and NIR-II window
ce:section.ce:para: The mice were randomly divided into five groups (five mice per group): (1) saline, (2) saline + 1064 nm laser (0.6 W cm−2), (3) CD/WS2 (600 μg mL−1), (4) CD/WS2 + 808 nm laser (0.2 W cm−2), and (5) CD/WS2 + 1064 nm laser (0.6 W cm−2). The 808 or 1064 nm laser was conducted after 24 h of intravenous injection. We recorded the volume of the tumors and body weight of the mice every other day and every day, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo deep-tissue PTT in the NIR-II windows
ce:section.ce:para: To compare the deep-tissue PTT ability of CD/WS2 HJs under 808 and 1064 nm laser irradiation, MG-63 tumors were covered by a 10 mm chicken breast tissue to simulate the clinical scenario. 200 μL of CD/WS2 solution (600 μg mL−1) or saline were intravenously injected into the MG-63 tumor-bearing mice. Then, tumors covered by a 10 mm chicken breast tissue were exposed to 1064 (0.8 W cm−2) or 808 nm (0.3 W cm−2) laser for 5 min. We recorded the volume of the tumors and body weight of the mice every other day and every day, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Histology measurements, blood analysis, and hemolysis assay
ce:section.ce:para:
Major organs were collected at the end of the treatment and stained by H&E using a digital microscope (Leica QWin). For hemolysis assay, human whole blood obtained from volunteer (5 mL) was added to calcium- and magnesium-free PBS solution (10 mL). Then, RBCs were isolated from serum by centrifuging at 500 g for 10 min and repeated for five times. After washed and diluted in 50 mL PBS, the RBC suspension (0.2 mL) was added to CD/WS2 solution (0.8 mL). The final concentration of CD/WS2 solutions ranges from 100 to 600 μg mL−1. The positive control and negative control group was DI water and PBS, respectively. Percent hemolysis was calculated using the following equation.

Human bone marrow stromal cells (hMSCs) were purchased from Cyagen Biosciences. We first performed the MTT assays to determine the cytotoxicity of WS2, CD/WS2, WS2 plus 1064 nm laser, and CD/WS2 plus 1064 nm laser. The hMSCs treated with CD/WS2 HJs (100 μg mL−1) were periodically irradiated using 1064 nm laser to maintain its temperature at 40.5 ± 0.5 °C for 5 min. We then investigated the effect of CD/WS2 HJs plus laser on the osteogenic differentiation of hMSCs. After 24 h incubation of hMSCs in 12-well plate, the osteoinductive media was added to replace the basal medium. hMSCs were then incubated with CD/WS2 HJs (100 µg mL−1) and periodically exposed to 1064 nm laser.

After cultured with WS2, CD/WS2, WS2 plus 1064 nm laser, and CD/WS2 plus 1064 nm laser in the osteogenic media for 7 and 14 d, hMSCs were lysed by the cell lysis buffer for the ALP activity assay. The lysed samples were centrifuged to collect the supernatant. Then, we used a BCA protein assay kit (Solarbio) to detect the total amount of intracellular protein content. Finally, the ALP activity was performed by Alkaline phosphatase assay kit (Nanjing Jiancheng Biological Engineering Institute, China) according to manufacturer’s instruction.

After cultured with WS2, CD/WS2, WS2 plus 1064 nm laser, and CD/WS2 plus 1064 nm laser in the osteogenic media for 14 d, hMSCs were fixed using paraformaldehyde (4%) and stained with alizarin red (Sigma, 2%) for 10 min. hMSCs were washed three times and imaged using an Olympus microscope. 10% of cetylpyridium chloride was added and the 570 nm absorbance was measured to quantify the coloration of ARS.

Real-time polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) was carried out to assess the osteogenic marker genes expression including ALP, RUNX2, OCN, HSP70. Total RNA from hMSCs treated with different samples for 7 days was extracted using MiniBEST Universal RNA Extraction Kit (Takara). Then, the RNA was reverse transcribed to complementary DNA (cDNA) according to the PrimeScript reagent Kit with gDNA Eraser (Takara). Finally, the RT-PCR was performed by TB Green Premix Ex Taq (Takara) according to manufacturer’s instruction. Sequences of the primers in this study were listed as following:
RUNX2 forward 5′-GGCCCACAAATCTCAGATCGTT-3′;
RUNX2 reverse 5′-CACTGGCGCTGCAACAAGAC-3′;
ALP forward 5′-AACACCACCCAGGGGAAC-3′;
ALP reverse 5′-GGTCACAATGCCCACAGATT-3′;
OCN forward 5′-GGCGCTACCTGTATCAATGG-3′;
OCN reverse 5′-TCAGCCAACTACTCACAGTC-3′;
HSP70 forward 5′-TGTTCCGTTTCCAGCCCCCAA-3′;
HSP70 reverse 5′-TAGTTGCTGCCTCTGTTCGGG-3′;
GAPDH forward 5′-ATGGGAAGCTGGTCATCAAC-3′;
GAPDH reverse 5′-GGATGCAGGGATGATGTTCT-3′.

hMSCs treated with WS2, CD/WS2, WS2 plus 1064 nm laser, and CD/WS2 plus 1064 nm laser for 7 days were lysed in cold RIPA buffer (Beyotime, P0013) containing 1 mM PMSF for 30 min and the extract was transferred to a micro-centrifuge tube. 20 µg of protein were loaded onto 12% SDS-PAGE and transferred to PVDF (Beyotime Biotechnology, FFP24) membranes. The PVDF membranes were then blocked with 5% BSA for 2 h and incubated with primary antibodies at 4 °C for 24 h. The following antibodies were used at a dilution of 1:1000 unless otherwise stated: anti-HSP70 (Abcam, Ab181606), anti-OCN (Abcam, Ab133612), anti-RUNX2 (Cst, 12556), anti-COL-1 (Abcam, Ab90395), anti-β-catenin (Cst, 8480), anti-GAPDH (Abcam, Ab8245, 1:5000). Subsequently, a secondary antibody, HRP-linked goat anti-rabbit IgG (Jackson, 115-035-003, 1:5000), was used to incubate with the PVDF membranes for 3 h. Finally, we utilized the enhanced chemiluminescent (ECL) detection systems to detect the protein level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NIR-II responsive CD/WS2 HJs
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 presented the rational design and synthetic strategy of CD/WS2 HJs through three steps. We first developed an ultrafast microwave-assisted hydrothermal method for the synthesis of CDs with excellent photothermal effect in the NIR-II window. As depicted in the structural model (Scheme 1), the pyrrolic N functionalized CDs show positively charged on its surface. Second, we utilized a liquid exfoliation method to synthesize the single-layer WS2 nanosheet from bulk WS2 by H2SO4 intercalation and ultrasonication. Finally, the 0D/2D CD/WS2 HJs were obtained through a simple electrostatic interaction after the positively charged CDs and negatively charged WS2 nanosheets were mixed and vigorously stirred under PBS (pH = 8.0) for 24 h. Due to a strong surface charge driven assembly, a stably dispersed dark brown solution of the CD/WS2 HJs were obtained.

Fig. 1 a revealed the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of NIR-II-responsive CDs. The average size of well-dispersed CDs was 10.6 ± 1.3 nm (inset in Fig. 1a). The distinguishable crystalline structure of CDs can be observed in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1b). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image in Fig. 1c showed that the CDs were nearly monodisperse and the average thickness was approximately 1.0 nm. For WS2 nanosheets, TEM (Fig. 1d) and AFM analysis (Fig. 1f) revealed that the average size and thickness of WS2 nanosheets were about 100–200 nm and 1.0–1.5 nm, respectively, indicating the formation of mono- and few-layer nanosheets from WS2 bulk. The interlayer spacing of WS2 nanosheets was measured to be 0.29 nm (Fig. 1e), which can be attributed to the (1 0 1) plane [34]. After assembling, the TEM and HRTEM image of CD/WS2 HJs showed that the CDs with a diameter of about 10 nm were well-distributed on WS2 nanosheets (Fig. 1g,h). AFM image of CD/WS2 HJs revealed that the thickness of WS2 nanosheets increased to 2.5–3.0 nm after CD loading (Fig. 1i), mainly due to the attachment of CDs on the surface of WS2 nanosheets. The average hydrodynamic sizes of WS2 nanosheets and CD/WS2 HJs were analyzed by dynamic light scattering (DLS) and determined to be 165 nm and 168 nm, respectively (Fig. S1a,b), indicating that the size of the WS2 nanosheets have no apparent change after CD loading. As depicted in Fig. S1c, the zeta potential of the WS2 nanosheets and CDs were −13.3 and 23.0 mV, respectively. Then, the zeta potential of obtained CD/WS2 HJs was determined to 12.1 mV, demonstrating the successful loading of CDs. The physiological stability of pristine WS2 nanosheets and CD/WS2 HJs was also investigated. Compared with pristine WS2 nanosheets, the obtained CD/WS2 HJs exhibit significantly enhanced physiological stability, without showing any agglomeration in PBS, DMEM culture medium, and blood after 3000 rpm centrifugation (Fig. S2).

The composition and structure of the CD/WS2 HJs were further investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD). As presented in Fig. 2 a, XRD pattern demonstrated that the diffraction peaks of as-prepared WS2 nanosheets were consistent with the hexagonal phase of WS2 (JCPDS card no. 87–2417) [35]. After CD loading, a new broad peak at around 21.2° can be observed in CD/WS2 HJs (Fig. 2b), which is consistent with the characteristic peak of pristine CDs (Fig. S3). The FTIR spectra further confirmed the existence of CDs on the WS2 nanosheets (Fig. S4). The chemical states of W and S in WS2 nanosheets were analyzed by the XPS spectra (Fig. 2c and S5). Two predominant peaks at 31.5 eV and 33.6 eV can be detected in the high-resolution W 4f spectrum, which could be ascribed to W 4f7/2 and W 4f5/2, respectively. S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 peaks at 162.3 and 161.2 eV in the high-resolution S 2p spectrum confirmed the presence of divalent sulfide ions (S2−). Apart from the existence of W and S, the survey XPS spectra of CD/WS2 HJs revealed another three typical peaks with high intensity: C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s at 284, 399, and 531 eV, respectively (Fig. 2d). The C–C, C–N, C–OH, graphitic N, pyrrole N, and O–H can be observed in the high-resolution C 1s (Fig. 2g), N 1s (Fig. 2h), and O 1s spectra (Fig. 2i), respectively. It should be noted that the chemical states of CD/WS2 HJs observed in C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s spectra were consistent with those of the pristine CDs (Fig. S6), confirming that the CDs were successfully loaded on the surface of WS2 nanosheets.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NIR-II enhanced photothermal effects of CD/WS2 HJs
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to investigate the enhanced photothermal effects of HJs, we first studied the absorption and fluorescence spectra of the CD/WS2 HJs by comparison with those of single CDs. The fluorescence spectrum of CDs revealed strong emission peak at 480 nm under the excitation wavelength at 420 nm (Fig. 3 a and S7a). The blue emission of CDs was correlated with the band edge transitions between the HOMO and LUMO gap. However, the CD/WS2 HJs (200 μg mL−1) exhibited significant PL quenching compared to the pristine CDs (200 μg mL−1). We next inspected the electronic structure to understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism of HJs. The CV curve of CDs was measured to determine its reduction potential (Ered) and LUMO energy level, which can be determined to be −0.81 V and −3.59 eV, respectively (Fig. S7b). Besides, the LUMO of WS2 was −3.80 eV according to previously reported [36], indicating an energy difference (0.21 eV) between CDs and WS2. Therefore, the electron on the conduction band (CB) of CDs could be transferred to that of WS2, resulting in significant PL quenching.

Fig. 3b revealed that the pristine CDs and WS2 both exhibit strong absorption ranging from NIR-II to visible region. The UV–vis-NIR absorption spectrum of CDs extending to the wide NIR region can be ascribed to the graphitic N doping, which can inject excess electrons into the unoccupied p* orbitals between the HOMO and LUMO gap and reduce the energies of the corresponding optical transitions, leading to the marked red-shift in their absorption spectrum [37,38]. Furthermore, compared to the pristine CDs (150 μg mL−1) and WS2 (50 μg mL−1), CD/WS2 HJs (200 μg mL−1) composed of CDs and WS2 exhibited enhanced absorption. Specifically, the absorbance of CD/WS2 HJs at 1064 nm is 0.65, which is much higher than that of CDs (0.16) and WS2 (0.21). To understand the mechanism of the enhanced absorption, the band gap of the CDs was detected to be 2.25 eV (Fig. S7c), which is wider than that of WS2 (2.0 eV) [39]. Under the NIR-II light exciting, the excited electrons in the valence band (VB) of CDs can be captured by the graphitic-N-induced defect states (Fig. 3c). Subsequently, the generated holes were rapidly transferred to the WS2 core, resulting that the p-type conduction of WS2 could be maintained for a long time. Furthermore, the localized surface plasmonic resonance (LSPR) effect could be induced in the WS2 core due to the abundant free carrier [40,41], which may lead to the enhanced absorption in the NIR-II region.

Motivated by the remarkably enhanced absorption of CD/WS2 HJs, we further investigated the photothermal performance of the CD/WS2 HJs in the NIR-II region. The temperature changes of CD/WS2 HJs, CDs, and WS2 at the same concentration (600 μg mL−1) under 1064 nm laser irradiation (0.6 W cm−2) were first compared. The temperature of the CD/WS2 HJs increased rapidly from 28.5 °C to 53.0 °C after irradiation for 5 min, while that of pure CDs and WS2 only increased to 48.1 and 45.0 °C, respectively (Fig. 3d), indicating the enhanced photothermal properties of CD/WS2 HJs. The in vitro IR imaging of CD/WS2, CDs and WS2 presented in Fig. 3j revealed the change of temperature during irradiation. Furthermore, the temperature variation curves of CD/WS2 with different loading ratios of CDs (50%, 100%, 150%, and 200%) were also investigated under the 1064 nm irradiation. Fig. 3e revealed a concentration-dependent manner, indicating the rapid and efficient energy conversion in the CD/WS2 HJs. Subsequently, the CD/WS2 (600 μg mL−1) were also exposed to 1064 nm laser at varied power density (0.4, 0.6, and 0.8 W cm−2). For example, a temperature increasing of 26.0 °C could be realized after 5 min irradiations (0.8 W cm−2) (Fig. 3f). Moreover, no significant temperature changes of the CD/WS2 solution can be observed even after ten cycles (Fig. 3g). To further investigate the photothermal stability of CD/WS2 HJs, we performed the UV–vis-NIR absorption spectra of CD/WS2 HJs before and after 1064 nm laser irradiation at varied power densities (0.4, 0.6, 0.8 W cm−2) for 60 min. As shown in Fig. S10, the CD/WS2 HJs showed no significant deterioration in its UV–vis-NIR absorption after continuous laser irradiation at different power densities, indicating that CDs cannot be released from CD/WS2 HJs after 1064 nm laser irradiation. Meanwhile, the enhanced photothermal performance of CD/WS2 HJs in the NIR-I region were also investigated by evaluating the concentration-dependent manner, power-dependent behavior, and photostability (Fig. S8a-c and S9).

The photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of CD/WS2 HJs was evaluated to verify its excellent photothermal conversion capability. According to the results of the absorbance, Tmax, and τs of CD/WS2 HJs (Fig. 3h-i), the η of the CD/WS2 HJs at 1064 nm was calculated to be 56.3%, while that of pristine WS2 was only 39.1%, confirming the synergistic enhanced photothermal properties of the CD/WS2 HJs. Moreover, the photothermal conversion efficiency of CD/WS2 HJs at 808 nm (0.2 W cm−2) was calculated to be 66.6% (Fig. S8c-d).

The deep-tissue photothermal properties of CD/WS2 HJs was also investigated in the in the NIR-I and NIR-II windows. We used the chicken breast tissue with varied thickness (2–10 mm) as a model biological tissue. Specifically, CD/WS2 HJs (600 μg mL−1) wrapped with chicken breast tissue were exposed to the 808 or 1064 nm laser at 0.3 or 0.8 W cm−2, respectively (Fig. 3k). Compared with the NIR-I laser, the CD/WS2 HJ solutions exhibit higher Tmax and less attenuation under the NIR-II laser (Fig. S11). The temperature increment of 22.0 °C under 1064 nm laser irradiation with the tissue depth of 10 mm could be observed in Fig. 3l, while the temperature increment of the CD/WS2 HJ solution only 8.5 °C under 808 nm laser irradiation. These results forcefully illustrated that the CD/WS2 HJs have a promising potential for deep-tissue PTT in the NIR-II window.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro NIR-II PTT effects of CD/WS2 HJs
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to synergistic enhanced photothermal properties, biocompatibility which was the primary requirement for the biomedical applications also needs to be carefully evaluated before its PTT application in vitro and in vivo. The cellular uptake of CD/WS2 HJs was first investigated using quantitative flow cytometry as previously reported [42,43]. CD/WS2 HJs exhibited relatively high efficiency of cell internalization after 2-h incubation at 37 °C (Fig. S12). MG-63 cells were incubated with CDs, WS2, and CD/WS2 HJs at the different concentrations without a laser irradiation for 24 h or 48 h to assess their cytotoxicity using MTT assays. CD/WS2 HJs exhibit excellent biocompatibility at all tested concentration (Fig. S13a-c).

Subsequently, we used CD/WS2 HJs as a PTT agent for osteosarcoma ablation in vitro. MG-63 cells were incubated with CDs, WS2, and CD/WS2 HJs and then exposed to a 1064 nm laser (0.6 W cm−2) for 5 min. We first investigated whether the 1064 nm irradiation could induce side effects. As shown in Fig. S14, no significant decline of cell viability was observed in MG-63 cells after incubation for 48 h with irradiation under the 1064 nm laser at varied power density (0.2, 0.4, 0.6, and 0.8 W cm−2) for 5 min. Fig. S13d-f revealed that the cell viability of the MG-63 cells decreases rapidly with increasing the concentration of the three sample. Specifically, the cell viability of MG-63 cells treated with CDs and WS2 plus 1064 nm laser was 25.0% and 45.9%, respectively. Nevertheless, the cell viability of MG-63 cells treated with CD/WS2 HJs (600 μg mL−1) was only 6% under the same irradiation condition. These findings indicated that the CD/WS2 HJs exhibit excellent photothermal properties and great promise in PTT treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CT imaging of CD/WS2 HJs for osteosarcoma diagnosis
ce:section.ce:para: Duo to the advantages of high density resolution and deep tissue penetration, CT imaging has been considered as a reliable technique for clinical diagnosis. We evaluated the CT imaging capacity of CD/WS2 HJs containing W element. Fig. 4 a revealed that significantly enhanced CT signal can be observed with the concentration of CD/WS2 HJs increased from 0 to 10 mg mL−1. The CT value was then calculated to be 23.6 HU L g−1 (Fig. 4b), which is significant higher than that of commercial iopromide (15.9 HU L/g) [44]. We then evaluated the feasibility of CD/WS2 HJs as a CT imaging contrast agent in vivo. After 24 h of intravenously injected with CD/WS2 HJs (1.0 mg mL−1, 200 μL), a remarkably enhanced CT signal was clearly observed at the tumor site compared to the uninjected nude mice (Fig. 4c), suggesting that the CD/WS2 HJs have an outstanding capability for in vivo CT imaging. More importantly, these results provided decisive evidence that our HJ nanostructure could be targeted to tumor areas due to the EPR effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo NIR-II PTT effects of CD/WS2 HJs
ce:section.ce:para: The real-time biodistribution and targeted efficacy of CD/WS2 HJs in MG-63 tumor-bearing mice was further investigated before the in vivo PTT. After intravenously injected of CD/WS2 HJs, major organs and tumors were harvested at varied time points (0, 6, 12, 24, and 48 h) and imaged. Fig. S15 revealed that the stronger fluorescence signals of CD/WS2 HJs were observed in tumor, providing direct evidence that CD/WS2 HJs were accumulated at the tumor.

Having confirmed the in vitro PTT efficiency and the tumor-specific drug delivery of CD/WS2 HJs, we next evaluated the in vivo PTT efficacy. The MG-63 tumor model was constructed by subcutaneously injecting MG-63 osteosarcoma cells. Five groups were divided as follows: group 1: saline; group 2: saline + 1064 nm laser; group 3: CD/WS2; group 4: CD/WS2 + 808 nm laser (0.2 W cm−2); group 5: CD/WS2 + 1064 nm laser (0.6 W cm−2). Based on the CT imaging results, we carried out the PTT treatment after 24 h of intravenously injected with CD/WS2. Fig. 5 g revealed that the temperature of the tumor treated with CD/WS2 plus 1064 nm laser irradiation rapidly increased to 57.0 °C, which was obvious higher than that of the saline group (39.5 °C) under the same irradiation conditions. The therapeutic effect of CD/WS2 HJs was evaluated by monitor the tumor volume and mice weight during the treatment periods. Notably, the growth of tumors in mice treated with CD/WS2 plus 808 or 1064 nm laser irradiation were completely inhibited (Fig. 5d-e). However, the volume of tumors in the other group were rapidly increased (Fig. 5a). No significant decrease in body weight of the mice could be observed after treatment with different groups (Fig. 5f), indicating that the CD/WS2 HJs induced no obvious acute toxicity. These results clearly indicated that CD/WS2 HJs offer excellent photothermal therapeutic effect against tumors.

We further performed deep tissue cancer therapy using CD/WS2 HJs as a PTT agent in the NIR-II window. The MG-63 tumors wrapped with 10-mm-thick chicken breast tissue was performed to simulation the clinical scenario (Fig. 5b). CD/WS2 HJ solutions were intravenously injected into the nude mice and then the 1064 nm laser was carried out. The tumors treated with CD/WS2 HJs were complete eradication without reoccurrence after the NIR-II PTT treatment, while the volume of tumors in NIR-I group exhibited a dramatic increase (Fig. 5c). These results indicated that CD/WS2 HJs afforded outstanding deep-tissue therapeutic effect in the NIR-II window, showing great promise in the clinic applications for tumor treatment with minimized side effects.

To further evaluate the potential side effects of CD/WS2 HJs, we studied the normal hematology parameters to confirm their biosafety. The normal hematology parameters including platelets (PLT), red blood cells (RBC), white blood cells (WBC), hemoglobin (HGB), mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH), and mean corpuscular volume (MCV) revealed no meaningful changes in the PTT treatment group and the control group (Fig. S16). Furthermore, we also investigated the influence of CD/WS2 HJs on hemolytic behavior of RBCs. Fig. S17 exhibited negligible hemolysis in the RBCs treated with varied concentrations of the CD/WS2 HJs. Additionally, the systemic toxicity of CD/WS2 HJs was also assessed by the H&E histological analysis of tissue sections from the main organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) after 18 d of treatment. No significant long-term toxicity of all organs during the treatment period can be observed in various groups (Fig. S18), suggesting the excellent safety of CD/WS2 HJs.

Having verified the excellent photothermal properties, we further investigated the capability of CD/WS2 HJs for enhancing new bone regeneration because bone defects could be induced during the treatment of osteosarcoma. The cytotoxicity of hMSCs cultured with WS2 and CD/WS2 HJs was first investigated via MTT assay to confirm their biocompatibility. No significantly cytotoxicity can be detected in the hMSCs treated with WS2 or CD/WS2 HJs for 24, 48, and 96 h, respectively (Fig. S19). Subsequently, the enhanced osteogenic differentiation of hMSCs in the presence of CD/WS2 HJs was investigated by evaluating the activity of alkaline phosphatase (ALP), which is a cell surface glycoprotein and an early phenotypic marker for osteogenic differentiation [45–47]. Fig. 6 a revealed the ALP activity in hMSCs treated with CD/WS2 was significantly increased by 2.1-fold compared to the control group, confirming that the presence of HJs enhanced osteogenic differentiation of hMSCs. Furthermore, according to previously reported, mild temperature stimulation can accelerate the differentiation of hMSCs toward the osteogenic lineage. For example, continuous incubation at 39 °C or repeated transient exposure to 39/41 °C greatly enhanced the ability of hMSCs to form mineralizing nodules [18]. Therefore, we further explored whether additional 1064 nm laser radiation could improve the osteogenic differentiation ability of CD/WS2 HJs. The hMSCs treated with CD/WS2 HJs (100 μg mL−1) were periodically exposed to 1064 nm laser to maintain its temperature at 40.5 ± 0.5 °C for 5 min. No significant cytotoxicity can be observed in the hMSCs treated with CD/WS2 HJs with the additional 1064 nm laser irradiation, indicating that the periodically 1064 nm laser irradiation has no effect on the growth of hMSCs. Fig. 6a exhibited that the ALP activity in hMSCs treated with CD/WS2 HJs upon 1064 nm laser irradiation was significantly increased by 3.3-fold, which was higher than that of CD/WS2 HJs without laser irradiation, demonstrating that photothermal stimulation could remarkably improve the osteogenic differentiation. However, no significant change of ALP activity can be observed in the hMSCs treated with WS2 with or without laser irradiation, indicating that pristine WS2 nanosheets cannot reach the required temperature (41 °C) under 1064 nm laser irradiation in the absence of CDs.

We next evaluated the amount of mineralized matrix to confirm the mineralization efficiency of hMSCs treat with CD/WS2 HJs. The mineralization efficiency can be analyzed by ARS staining which is a marker for inorganic calcium [27,48]. In the CD/WS2 plus 1064 nm laser irradiation group, we observed very specific cellular organization, resulting in the deposition of extracellular matrix (ECM) in concentrated circular regions (arrows in the Fig. S20). After alizarin red straining, the hMSCs treated with CD/WS2 under 1064 nm laser irradiation showed a highly dense network of mineralized nodules throughout the construct (Fig. 6d), which is significantly higher than that of CD/WS2 without laser irradiation, indicating that CD/WS2 HJs could promote osteogenic differentiation of hMSCs upon 1064 nm laser irradiation. Moreover, no significant change of the efficiency of the mineralization stage was observed in the hMSCs treated with WS2 with or without laser irradiation, demonstrating that the temperature required to promote osteogenic differentiation cannot be achieved without CDs. The digital images of the ARS stained sample further indicated that CD/WS2 under 1064 nm laser irradiation significantly increased the amount of mineralized matrix (Fig. 6c). Subsequently, spectrophotometric quantification analysis further confirmed the improved mineralized matrix by eluting ARS from stained mineral deposits with cetylpyridium chloride. Compared with CD/WS2 without laser irradiation, the amount of mineralized matrix of the CD/WS2 HJs under 1064 nm laser irradiation was increased by 150% (Fig. 6b), indicating that mild temperature plays a crucial role in accelerating osteogenic differentiation.

The osteogenic differentiation of hMSCs is commonly accompanied with the expression of a series of genetic marker sequences, therefore, we evaluated the mRNA expression of osteoblast gene markers, such as osteocalcin (OCN, a later marker for osteogenic differentiation) [49], Runt-related transcription factor-2 (RUNX2, expressed only in mineralized tissues) [50], and ALP. As demonstrated by the RT-PCR assay (Fig. S21), the mRNA expression of RUNX2, OCN, and ALP in the hMSCs treated with CD/WS2 HJs showed prominent upregulation. Furthermore, compared to CD/WS2 HJs group, 1.42, 1.35, and 1.45-fold increases of the mRNA expression of RUNX2, OCN, and ALP can be detected in the hMSCs treated with CD/WS2 HJs upon 1064 nm laser irradiation, respectively, indicating that photothermal stimulation could improve the bone regeneration.

In addition to the RT-PCR assays, we further evaluated the protein levels of OCN and RUNX2 by western blotting. As shown in Fig. 6e, compared to CD/WS2 HJs without laser irradiation, the expression levels of RUNX2 and OCN in the hMSCs treated with CD/WS2 under 1064 nm irradiation were obvious increased. Moreover, the protein level of type I collagen (Col-I), which is one of the osteogenic markers for differentiation and maturation of hMSCs [51], was also assessed. Fig. 6e revealed that the expression of Col-I of CD/WS2 HJs plus 1064 nm laser irradiation treated hMSCs were much higher than that of other group. Furthermore, we next investigated the expressions of β-catenin, which is related to Wnt signaling pathway and plays an important role in the osteoblastic differentiation [52–55]. The β-catenin level of hMSCs cultured with CD/WS2 plus 1064 nm laser was higher than other groups (Fig. 6e), indicating that the activation of Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway could obvious promote osteogenic differentiation.

It should be noted that the CD/WS2 HJs plus 1064 nm laser irradiation group exhibited the best effect on promoting osteogenic differentiation. To illustrate the underlying mechanism of enhance osteogenic differentiation by hyperthermia stimulation, we examined the expression of heat shock protein (HSP70), which plays a key role in promoting osteogenesis [56]. Fig. S21 revealed that the gene levels of HSP70 of hMSCs treated with CD/WS2 plus 1064 nm laser irradiation was remarkable up-regulated. Besides evaluating the levels of genes, we further evaluated the protein levels of HSP70 by western blotting. The HSP70 level of CD/WS2 plus laser irradiation group is significant higher than that of CD/WS2 without laser irradiation group (Fig. 6e), clearly suggesting that the hyperthermia generated by CD/WS2 under 1064 nm laser irradiation can up-regulate the expressions of HSP70 and hence promote osteogenic differentiation.

In summary, bifunctional CD/WS2 HJs were successfully fabricated by assembling the positively charged CDs and negatively charged WS2 nanosheets through a simple electrostatic interaction. The obtained CD/WS2 HJs exhibited excellent deep-tissue photothermal effect to ablate osteosarcoma under 1064 nm laser irradiation (0.6 W cm−2) in the presence of the 10-mm-thick additional tissue. Furthermore, CD/WS2 HJs upon periodic and low intensity 1064 nm laser irradiation greatly accelerate the osteogenic differentiation of hMSCs, during which the up-regulation of heat shock protein expression plays a vital role. Our results indicated that CD/WS2 HJs can not only achieve complete bone-tumor elimination without recurrence through localized photothermal therapy, but also repair treatment-induced bone defects by means of hyperthermia-enhanced bone regeneration, showing great application potential in cancer therapy and bone-tissue engineering.
